content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 27, 1994

Carl Waggoner
Law Offices of Kroll,
Loeffler & Waggoner
611 Thirteen Street
Post Office Box 3489
Modesto, CA 95353-3489

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-311

Dear Mr. Waggoner:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Stephen McKee, Lathrop City Councilmember, regarding his
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and is a follow-up to an earlier
request for advice, Waggoner Advice Letter, No. I-90-529.

In 1991, in response to your prior written request for advice
referenced above, we advised you that Mayor McKee could not
participate in any city decision which would have a material
financial effect on Verner Construction or I.E.T. and
Kathleen Verner. This was because Mayor McKee and his father had
entered into an option to purchase agreement with the Verners in
1988 and as a result of that agreement he and his father had
received payments from the Verners. Specifically, upon execution
of the agreement in 1988, Councilmember McKee and his father
received $40,000; in March 1990, they received an additional
$25,000.²

_______________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    There is some confusion regarding the facts. For example, in
your letter to Councilmember McKee dated April 18, 1993 (an
attachment), you stated that John Verner was paying Councilmember
McKee an annual amount in excess of $250 for his option in the
McKees' real property. Yet, in your letter of August 9, 1993, you
stated that Councilmember McKee and his father received their last
payment from the Verners in 1991, whereas in your most recent
letter of January 13, 1994, you stated that the last payment was
received in 1990.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-311
Page 2


     Under all the facts, including the terms and conditions of
the contract and various addendums between the parties, the
Commission was of the opinion that reasonable grounds existed for
the expectation of receiving income from the Verners in the
future.  Thus, John and Kathleen Verner and Verner Construction
were considered sources of promised income to Mayor McKee.
(Wagoner Advice Letter, supra.)

     In your recent letter, you inform us that Mr. Verner has now
terminated in writing his right to exercise the option to purchase
Councilmember McKee's property under the Joint Venture Agreement
signed in June 1988.3  Specifically, the notice of termination
provides that Councilmember McKee is released from all conditions
of the contract and that all moneys released as of June 8, 1993,
are forfeited.

     Pursuant to Section 87103(c), a public official may not
participate in a governmental decision that will have a material
financial effect on a source of income for 12 months after the
receipt of income.  The question you raise now is whether
Councilmember McKee is required to wait an additional 12 months
after the termination of the option agreement before he may
participate in a decision that will have a material financial
effect on Verner Construction or its owners, John and Kathleen
Verner.

     If the assumptions contained in footnote 3 herein are
accurate, and assuming the written notice of termination does in
fact contractually extinguish the Verners' rights and obligations
to purchase Councilmember McKee's and his father's property so
that the Verners do not have an interest in the McKees' real
property, then Councilmember McKee would no longer have a
reasonable expectation of future income or "promised income" from
that source.  Accordingly, Councilmember McKee would no longer be
required to disqualify himself from city decisions where his only
financial interest in the decision is Verner Construction or John
or Kathleen Verner.


3    As noted herein, there were numerous contracts entered into
between the parties.  For purposes of this letter, we are assuming
that the written notice of termination executed by Mr. Verner
extinguishes all contractual rights and obligations between ALL of
the parties, including Councilmember McKee's father and Kathleen
Verner.  We note no reference is made to Councilmember McKee's
father in the termination notice, though he is a co-owner of the
subject real property and was a party to all previous contracts,
including the option in question.  We also note that the notice
was not co-signed by Kathleen Verner.  If our assumptions are
incorrect, and, for example, the Verners still have an option or
interest in the McKees' property, or still have some type of
financial obligation to the McKees, then our advice would differ
and the conclusion contained in this letter cannot be relied upon.

Our File No. A-93-311
Page 3


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

Deanne Stone
By: Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel




4
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember McKee must wait an additional 12 months after termination of an option agreement with Verner Construction before participating in city decisions affecting that entity, given that he previously received payments under the agreement.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: If the written termination notice validly extinguishes all contractual rights and obligations between the parties, Councilmember McKee would no longer have a reasonable expectation of future income from the Verners and could participate in decisions affecting Verner Construction without disqualification, provided the assumptions in the letter are accurate.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC response to prior advice request (follow-up to A-90-529). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but question and conclusion can be synthesized from body text. Letter contains important caveats about assumptions regarding completeness of termination and potential issues with missing signatories. Date discrepancy noted: document dated January 27, 1994 but references letter of January 13, 1994.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Councilmember McKee must wait an additional 12 months after termination of an option agreement with Verner Construction before participating in city decisions affecting that entity, given that he previously received payments under the agreement.

CONCLUSION: If the written termination notice validly extinguishes all contractual rights and obligations between the parties, Councilmember McKee would no longer have a reasonable expectation of future income from the Verners and could participate in decisions affecting Verner Construction without disqualification, provided the assumptions in the letter are accurate.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Councilmember McKee may participate in city decisions affecting Verner Construction and its owners after a termination of an option agreement, provided the termination fully extinguishes all contractual rights and obligations and the assumptions outlined in the letter are correct.
